lesson.title: Causative vs active meaning
lesson.body: Compare the active I painted my house (I did it myself) with the causative I had my house painted (a professional did it). Confusing the two is a common error when describing paid services.

head: Meaning
Example
rows: They are getting their kitchen renovated.

items.en: She has her hair cut every six weeks.
items.note: Present simple causative — a regular arrangement.

items.en: We had our house painted last summer.
items.note: Past simple causative — a professional did the work.

items.en: I need to get my laptop repaired — the screen is cracked.
items.note: "Get" causative, informal.

items.en: By next week, we will have the whole house redecorated.
items.note: Future causative.

items.en: The company has its data backed up automatically every night.
items.note: Causative used for an automated process, not only people.

lesson.body: In Speaking Part 1/2, describing services (haircuts, repairs, deliveries) naturally calls for the causative. Using it correctly, rather than the plain active voice, shows precise control of a less common structure valued for band 7+ grammatical range.

quiz.prompt: She ___ her hair cut every six weeks.
quiz.why: The causative structure uses "have" (or "get") + object + past participle.

quiz.prompt: We ___ (have) our house painted last summer.
quiz.why: The past simple causative uses "had" + object + past participle.

quiz.prompt: I need to ___ my laptop repaired — the screen is cracked.
quiz.why: "Get" + object + past participle is a common informal causative form.

quiz.prompt: They are ___ (get) their office redecorated this month.
quiz.why: The present continuous causative uses "getting" + object + past participle for an ongoing arrangement.

quiz.prompt: Compare: "He fixed the car himself" vs "He ___ the car fixed by a mechanic."
quiz.why: "Had" + object + past participle shows that someone else (the mechanic) did the work.

quiz.prompt: She ___ (have) her nails done before the wedding.
quiz.why: The past simple causative describes a service arranged before the event.

quiz.prompt: Which sentence means a professional did the work?
quiz.why: "Had the windows cleaned" is the causative form, showing someone else did the cleaning.

quiz.prompt: By next week, we ___ (have) the whole house redecorated.
quiz.why: The future causative uses "will have" + object + past participle.

quiz.prompt: The company ___ its data backed up automatically every night.
quiz.why: The causative "have" also applies to automated processes, not only people.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite using a causative structure: "A mechanic services her car every year."
answers: She has her car serviced every year.
She gets her car serviced every year.
She has her car serviced every year
She gets her car serviced every year
quiz.why: The causative shifts focus from the mechanic (agent) to the person arranging the service.

title: Question Tags & Indirect Questions
blurb: Add tags to check information, and soften direct questions with polite indirect forms.

lesson.body: Two structures make questions sound more natural or more polite: question tags (to check or confirm information) and indirect questions (softer and more formal, common for requests to strangers or in formal writing).

lesson.title: Question tags
lesson.body: A positive statement takes a negative tag; a negative statement takes a positive tag. The tag repeats the auxiliary or modal from the sentence (or uses do/does/did for simple tenses): She is a teacher, isn't she? / They haven't finished, have they? / You like coffee, don't you?

lesson.title: Special cases
lesson.body: I am → aren't I?; imperatives take will you?; Let's takes shall we?; there is/are takes isn't there?/aren't there?.

lesson.title: Indirect questions
lesson.body: Begin with a polite frame (Could you tell me..., Do you know..., I wonder...) followed by statement word order — no auxiliary inversion. Yes/no questions are embedded with if/whether: Could you tell me where the station is? NOT where is the station.

head: Direct question
Indirect question
rows: Could you tell me where the station is?
Do you know if the museum is open today?
What time does the train leave?
I wonder what time the train leaves.

items.en: She's finishing her degree next year, isn't she?
items.note: Tag mirrors the auxiliary "is" in the statement.

items.en: Let's take a short break, shall we?
items.note: Special tag for "Let's".

items.en: Could you tell me where the nearest bank is?
items.note: Indirect wh-question — statement word order.

items.en: Do you know if the shop is open on Sundays?
items.note: Indirect yes/no question with "if".

items.en: They didn't call yesterday, did they?
items.note: Negative statement takes a positive tag.

lesson.body: Natural question tags (...isn't it?) make Speaking responses sound fluent and idiomatic. In formal Writing or Speaking, indirect questions (I'd like to know whether...) signal politeness and control of complex sentence structure — both reward band 7+ range.

quiz.prompt: She's finishing her degree next year, ___?
quiz.why: The main clause uses "is finishing" (present continuous), so the tag must mirror that auxiliary: isn't she.

quiz.prompt: You haven't seen this report before, ___ you?
quiz.why: A negative statement with "haven't" takes a positive tag using the same auxiliary: have you?

quiz.prompt: Let's take a short break, ___?
quiz.why: "Let's" always takes the special tag "shall we?"

quiz.prompt: I'm right about this, ___ I?
quiz.why: "I am" takes the irregular tag "aren't I?"

quiz.prompt: Could you tell me ___?
quiz.why: Indirect questions use statement word order, not auxiliary inversion.

quiz.prompt: Do you know ___ (the shop/be) open on Sundays?
quiz.why: An indirect yes/no question is introduced by "if" or "whether" plus statement word order.

quiz.prompt: They didn't call yesterday, ___?
quiz.why: A negative statement with "didn't" takes a positive tag using the same auxiliary: did they?

quiz.prompt: There isn't any evidence for this claim, ___ there?
quiz.why: A negative statement with "there isn't" takes a positive tag: is there?

quiz.prompt: Choose the most polite indirect question to ask a stranger at a station.
options: Tell me where the ticket office is.
Could you tell me where the ticket office is?
quiz.why: A polite frame ("Could you tell me...") plus statement word order is the standard indirect, polite form.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite as an indirect question: "What time does the museum close?"
answers: Could you tell me what time the museum closes?
Do you know what time the museum closes?
Could you tell me what time the museum closes
Do you know what time the museum closes
quiz.why: A polite frame plus statement word order (the museum closes) forms the indirect question.

title: Linking Words & Cohesion
blurb: Connect ideas clearly with contrast, cause and result linkers — plus so/such/too/enough.

lesson.body: Linking words connect clauses and sentences to signal contrast, cause and effect, or result — essential for IELTS Coherence and Cohesion. Different linkers need different grammar and punctuation.

lesson.title: Contrast connectors
lesson.body: Although/even though + a full clause: Although it was raining, they continued. Despite/in spite of + a noun or -ing form (never a full clause): Despite the rain, they continued. However starts a new sentence, followed by a comma.

lesson.title: Cause & result
lesson.body: Because + a full clause; because of + a noun. So introduces a result within one sentence; therefore/as a result are more formal and usually start a new sentence, followed by a comma.

lesson.title: So/such...that; too/enough
lesson.body: So + adjective/adverb + that vs such + (a/an) + adjective + noun + that both introduce a result: so difficult that / such a difficult test that. Too + adjective + to-infinitive shows an excessive, negative result; adjective + enough + to-infinitive shows sufficiency.

lesson.title: So vs such / too vs enough

head: Pattern
Example
rows: The test was so difficult that many failed.
It was such a difficult test that many failed.
The test was too difficult to finish.
She wasn't old enough to vote.

items.en: Despite the heavy rain, the match continued.
items.note: "Despite" + noun phrase, no full clause.

items.en: Although it was raining heavily, the match continued.
items.note: "Although" + a full clause.

items.en: Sales fell sharply. As a result, the company cut costs.
items.note: "As a result" starts a new sentence, followed by a comma.

items.en: The exam was so difficult that half the class failed.
items.note: "So" + adjective + "that" clause.

items.en: The coffee was too hot to drink immediately.
items.note: "Too" + adjective + to-infinitive shows an excessive result.

items.en: She wasn't experienced enough to lead the project.
items.note: Adjective + "enough" + to-infinitive shows insufficiency.

lesson.body: Band 7+ Coherence and Cohesion rewards a range of linkers used accurately, not just "and/but/so" repeated. Misusing "despite" with a full clause (Despite it was raining...) is a very common, heavily penalised error — always follow despite/in spite of with a noun or -ing form.

quiz.prompt: ___ the heavy rain, the match continued.
quiz.why: "Despite" is followed directly by a noun phrase ("the heavy rain"), not a full clause.

quiz.prompt: ___ it was raining, the match continued.
quiz.why: "Although"/"even though" is followed by a full clause, unlike "despite".

quiz.prompt: The exam was ___ difficult that half the class failed.
quiz.why: "So" + adjective + "that" introduces a result clause.

quiz.prompt: It was ___ a difficult exam that half the class failed.
quiz.why: "Such" + a/an + adjective + noun + "that" introduces a result clause.

quiz.prompt: The coffee was ___ hot to drink immediately.
quiz.why: "Too" + adjective + to-infinitive expresses an excessive, negative result.

quiz.prompt: She wasn't experienced ___ to lead the project.
quiz.why: Adjective + "enough" + to-infinitive expresses sufficiency (or, negated, insufficiency).

quiz.prompt: Sales fell sharply. ___, the company cut costs.
quiz.why: "As a result" formally links a cause in one sentence to a result in the next.

quiz.prompt: The flight was delayed ___ (because) bad weather.
quiz.why: "Because of" is followed by a noun phrase, unlike "because", which needs a full clause.

quiz.prompt: ___ studying hard, she failed the exam.
quiz.why: "Despite" is followed by a gerund ("studying"), not a full clause like "although" would need.

quiz.prompt: Combine using "so...that": "The music was very loud. We couldn't hear each other."
answers: The music was so loud that we couldn't hear each other.
The music was so loud that we could not hear each other.
The music was so loud that we couldn't hear each other
The music was so loud that we could not hear each other
quiz.why: "So" + adjective + "that" links the cause (loud music) to the result (couldn't hear).

title: Inversion & Emphasis
blurb: Add emphasis with negative adverbial inversion, conditional inversion and emphatic do.

lesson.body: Inversion moves the auxiliary verb before the subject to create emphasis, most often after a negative or restrictive adverbial, in formal conditional sentences, and with the emphatic auxiliary do. It is a hallmark of band 8+ academic and formal writing.

lesson.title: Negative & restrictive adverbial inversion
lesson.body: When a negative or restrictive adverbial (never, rarely, seldom, little, under no circumstances, not only) opens the sentence, the auxiliary comes before the subject: Never have I seen such enthusiasm. Not only did sales rise, but profits also doubled. Under no circumstances should the data be altered.

lesson.title: Conditional inversion
lesson.body: In formal style, if can be dropped and the auxiliary inverted with the subject instead: Were I to accept, I would relocate immediately. Had she known the risks, she would not have invested. Should you require further details, please contact the office.

lesson.title: Emphatic do/does/did
lesson.body: Adding do/does/did before the base verb stresses a positive statement, often countering doubt or contradicting an assumption: I do believe the plan will succeed. She did submit the report on time.

head: Trigger
Example
rows: Rarely does the committee meet twice a month.
Not only did costs fall, but quality also improved.
Had the warning been heeded, the loss would have been smaller.
Were the policy adopted, unemployment would fall.
Should any issues arise, contact support immediately.

items.en: Never have I read such a persuasive argument.
items.note: “Never” fronted → the auxiliary “have” inverts with the subject “I”.

items.en: Not only did the company cut emissions, but it also increased output.
items.note: “Not only” + inversion; “but also” links the second clause.

items.en: Had the government invested earlier, the crisis might have been avoided.
items.note: “Had”-inversion replaces “If the government had invested earlier”.

items.en: Under no circumstances should applicants submit incomplete forms.
items.note: A restrictive negative adverbial triggers inversion with “should”.

items.en: I do think the proposal deserves serious consideration.
items.note: Emphatic “do” stresses a positive opinion.

lesson.body: For band 8+ writing, use one inverted structure per essay for impact rather than several — overuse sounds unnatural. In speaking, emphatic do (I do think…) is far more natural than negative-adverbial inversion, which belongs mainly in formal writing.

quiz.prompt: ___ have I seen such a compelling presentation.
quiz.why: “Never” fronted for emphasis requires inversion of the auxiliary “have” and the subject “I”.

quiz.prompt: Not only ___ costs fall, but quality also improved.
quiz.why: “Not only” inversion in the past requires the auxiliary “did” before the base verb “fall”.

quiz.prompt: ___ she known about the delay, she would have rescheduled the meeting.
quiz.why: “Had she known” = “If she had known”, with the auxiliary fronted for formal conditional inversion.

quiz.prompt: ___ any problems occur, please contact the technical support team immediately.
quiz.why: “Should” + subject + base verb inverts “If any problems should occur” for a formal, polite conditional.

quiz.prompt: I ___ believe that the results, though modest, are statistically significant.
quiz.why: Emphatic “do” + base verb stresses a positive claim, often countering doubt.

quiz.prompt: ___ (rare) does the panel reach a unanimous decision on the first vote.
quiz.why: “Rarely” fronted as a restrictive adverbial triggers inversion with “does”.

quiz.prompt: Under no circumstances ___ students use mobile phones during the exam.
quiz.why: “Under no circumstances” already carries the negative meaning, so the following clause must be positive: “should”, not “shouldn't”.

quiz.prompt: ___ (little) did the researchers expect such dramatic results.
quiz.why: “Little” as a negative-restrictive adverbial triggers inversion: “Little did they expect…”.

quiz.prompt: ___ the proposal approved, funding would begin immediately.
quiz.why: Formal conditional inversion drops “if” and fronts “were”: “Were the proposal approved…” = “If the proposal were approved…”.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite using inversion: "If the company had known about the defect, it would have issued a recall."
answers: Had the company known about the defect, it would have issued a recall.
Had the company known about the defect, it would have issued a recall
quiz.why: “Had”-inversion replaces “If the company had known…” in formal style.

title: Cleft Sentences
blurb: Highlight the most important part of a sentence with it-clefts and wh-clefts.

lesson.body: Cleft sentences split a single idea into two clauses to put special focus on one element. The two main types are it-clefts (It was Maria who…) and wh-clefts (What surprised everyone was…), both common in academic writing and formal speech for emphasis.

lesson.title: It-clefts
lesson.body: It + be + focused element + who/that + rest of clause highlights a subject, object, time or place: It was the marketing team that launched the campaign. The focused element can be negated for contrast: It wasn't the price that put customers off, but the delivery time.

lesson.title: Wh-clefts (what-clefts)
lesson.body: What + clause + be + focused element highlights an idea, often at the end of the sentence for maximum emphasis: What the report reveals is a sharp decline in exports. What surprised researchers was the speed of the change.

lesson.title: Reversed wh-clefts & all-clefts
lesson.body: The focused element can come first instead: A shortage of housing is what caused the price rise. All can replace what to mean “the only thing”: All she wants is a fair hearing.

head: Pattern
Example
rows: It was the government that funded the project.
It was funding that the project lacked.
What the data shows is a steady rise in demand.
A steady rise in demand is what the data shows.

items.en: It was the final question that decided the debate.
items.note: It-cleft focuses on “the final question” as the cause.

items.en: It wasn't the cost that worried investors, but the uncertainty.
items.note: A negated it-cleft sets up an explicit contrast.

items.en: What concerns economists most is the rising cost of borrowing.
items.note: Wh-cleft places the focused idea after “is”.

items.en: A lack of affordable housing is what drove young people from the city.
items.note: Reversed wh-cleft puts the focused idea first.

items.en: All the committee wants is a clear, evidence-based proposal.
items.note: “All” + clause + be narrows focus to a single thing.

lesson.body: Cleft sentences let you control which idea receives stress without relying on spoken intonation, making them valuable in Task 2 essays and Speaking Part 3 for signposting your main point: What this suggests is that… is a strong, natural way to introduce an opinion.

quiz.prompt: ___ was the delayed shipment that caused the complaints, not the product quality.
quiz.why: It-cleft structure “It was X that…” focuses on “the delayed shipment” as the cause.

quiz.prompt: What surprised the researchers most ___ the speed of the change.
quiz.why: In a wh-cleft, “be” agrees with the focused noun phrase that follows; “the speed” is singular, so “was”.

quiz.prompt: ___ the committee needs is more time to consider the evidence.
quiz.why: “All + clause + be” narrows the focus to “the only thing”, here more time.

quiz.prompt: It wasn't the price ___ put customers off, but the long delivery time.
quiz.why: A negated it-cleft (“It wasn't X that…”) sets up the contrast completed by “but the long delivery time”.

quiz.prompt: A shortage of skilled workers is ___ has slowed the project's progress.
quiz.why: Reversed wh-cleft: noun phrase + is + what + clause.

quiz.prompt: ___ the survey reveals is a marked increase in remote working.
quiz.why: Wh-cleft opener: “What + clause + be + focused element”.

quiz.prompt: It was not until 2015 ___ the company turned a profit.
quiz.why: “It + be + not until + time + that…” is a common it-cleft pattern for emphasising when something happened.

quiz.prompt: It is the quality of the evidence, ___ the quantity, that determines a strong argument.
quiz.why: The parenthetical “not the quantity” contrasts with the focused element “the quality of the evidence”.

quiz.prompt: ___ (need) most is a coordinated policy response, not further debate.
quiz.why: Wh-cleft opener “What is needed…” focuses on the coordinated policy response as the required thing.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite as an it-cleft to focus on "the new manager": "The new manager introduced these changes."
answers: It was the new manager who introduced these changes.
It was the new manager who introduced these changes
It was the new manager that introduced these changes.
It was the new manager that introduced these changes
quiz.why: It-cleft “It was X who/that…” shifts focus onto “the new manager”.

title: Subjunctive & Unreal Past
blurb: Use the mandative subjunctive and unreal past forms for formal recommendations, wishes and hypothetical comparisons.

lesson.body: The subjunctive uses the base form of the verb (not the third-person -s) after verbs and expressions of recommendation, and an unreal past (were, not was) after hypothetical expressions such as as if and would rather. Both are markers of formal, precise English.

lesson.title: Mandative subjunctive
lesson.body: After verbs like recommend, suggest, insist, demand, propose and expressions like it is essential/vital/important that, the following clause uses the base form for every subject, with no -s and no change for past time: The committee recommends that the policy be reviewed. It is essential that she arrive on time.

lesson.title: Were-subjunctive & as if/as though
lesson.body: Were replaces was in formal hypotheticals for all persons: If I were in charge, I would act differently. As if/as though + past simple describes an unreal comparison in the present: He talks as if he were an expert. + past perfect describes an unreal comparison about the past: She looked as though she had seen a ghost.

lesson.title: Would rather & it's time
lesson.body: Would rather + subject + past simple expresses a preference about someone else's present/future action: I would rather you told me now. It's (high) time + subject + past simple expresses that something should already be happening: It's time the report was finished.

head: Pattern
Structure
Example
rows: The doctor insisted that he rest.
if / as if / as though + were
She acts as if she were the manager.
I'd rather you didn't mention it.

items.en: The board recommended that the budget be increased.
items.note: Mandative subjunctive: base form “be” after “recommended that”.

items.en: It is vital that every participant sign the consent form.
items.note: “Sign”, not “signs” — the subjunctive ignores third-person -s.

items.en: He speaks as if he were fluent in five languages.
items.note: Were-subjunctive after “as if” for an unreal present comparison.

items.en: I would rather you arrived a little earlier next time.
items.note: Would rather + past simple expresses a preference about someone else's action.

items.en: It's high time the committee made a final decision.
items.note: It's (high) time + past simple implies the action is overdue.

lesson.body: For band 8+ writing, the mandative subjunctive after formal verbs (recommend, propose, demand) sounds noticeably more sophisticated than a modal paraphrase; reserve were-subjunctive and would rather mainly for hypothetical or polite contexts, not everyday facts.

quiz.prompt: The professor insisted that the essay ___ resubmitted.
quiz.why: Mandative subjunctive after “insisted that” uses the base form “be”, regardless of tense.

quiz.prompt: It is essential that each candidate ___ the form before Friday.
quiz.why: Mandative subjunctive after “it is essential that” takes the base form “submit” for every subject.

quiz.prompt: She acts as if she ___ the only qualified person in the room.
quiz.why: “As if” introducing an unreal present comparison uses the subjunctive “were”, not the present tense “is”.

quiz.prompt: He behaves as if he ___ (be) responsible for the entire department.
quiz.why: “As if” introduces an unreal comparison; “were” is the traditional subjunctive form, though “was” is common in informal speech.

quiz.prompt: The regulations require that every applicant ___ proof of residence.
quiz.why: Mandative subjunctive after “require that” takes the base form “provide”, not the third-person -s form.

quiz.prompt: It is important that the results ___ (verify) independently before publication.
quiz.why: Mandative subjunctive after “it is important that” uses the base form of “be” + past participle for a passive meaning: “be verified”.

quiz.prompt: I would rather you ___ this to anyone right now.
quiz.why: “Would rather + subject + past simple” expresses a preference about someone else's present/future action.

quiz.prompt: It's high time the report ___ (finish).
quiz.why: “It's (high) time” + past simple describes something that should already have happened; here the passive “was finished”.

quiz.prompt: If she ___ (be) in charge, she would reorganise the whole department.
quiz.why: Hypothetical “if” clause: “were” is the traditional subjunctive for all persons; “was” is a common informal alternative.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite using a mandative subjunctive: "The dean insists that all staff must attend the meeting."
answers: The dean insists that all staff attend the meeting.
The dean insists that all staff attend the meeting
quiz.why: Mandative subjunctive drops the modal “must” and uses the bare base form “attend” after “insists that”.

title: Advanced Modality
blurb: Speculate about the past, express criticism and hedge claims with advanced modal patterns.

lesson.body: Beyond basic ability, permission and obligation, modal verbs combine with have + past participle to speculate about the past, criticise past actions, and hedge academic claims. These patterns are essential for nuanced Task 2 argumentation.

lesson.title: Past deduction
lesson.body: Must have + V3 expresses a confident positive deduction about the past; can't/couldn't have + V3 expresses a confident negative deduction; might/could/may have + V3 expresses an uncertain possibility: The results must have been affected by sampling error. She can't have finished already — it's only been ten minutes.

lesson.title: Criticism & regret
lesson.body: Should have/shouldn't have + V3 criticises a past action that did or didn't happen: The government should have consulted experts first. Needn't have + V3 means an action happened but was unnecessary: She needn't have worried; the results were fine. This differs from didn't need to, which usually means the action was not done because it was unnecessary.

lesson.title: Hedged academic modality
lesson.body: Combining a modal with a hedging adverb softens a claim for academic caution: may well, might arguably, could conceivably: The trend may well continue if current policies persist. Would seem to hedges an inference: The data would seem to support this hypothesis.

head: Meaning
Form
Example
rows: He must have missed the announcement.
You shouldn't have deleted the file.
We needn't have booked in advance.

items.en: The witness must have seen something, given how nervous she looked.
items.note: Must have + V3 = confident positive deduction about the past.

items.en: He can't have read the instructions carefully, or he wouldn't have made that mistake.
items.note: Can't have + V3 = confident negative deduction.

items.en: The board should have anticipated the market downturn.
items.note: Should have + V3 criticises a past inaction.

items.en: You needn't have brought an umbrella; it never rained.
items.note: Needn't have + V3 = an unnecessary action that was still carried out.

items.en: The policy may well reduce emissions, though further evidence is needed.
items.note: A hedged modal softens the claim for academic caution.

lesson.body: For band 8+ speaking and writing, layering a hedge onto a modal (may well, would arguably) signals sophisticated caution without sounding vague — far stronger than repeating “maybe” or “probably”.

quiz.prompt: Judging by the empty glasses, several guests ___ before we arrived.
quiz.why: Must have + past participle expresses a confident deduction about a completed past action.

quiz.prompt: She left only twenty minutes ago, so she ___ have finished the marathon by now.
quiz.why: Given the short time elapsed, a confident negative deduction is needed: “can't have finished”.

quiz.prompt: You ___ have told me the meeting was cancelled — I got up at 5 a.m. for nothing!
quiz.why: “Should have + V3” criticises the past action of not telling the speaker sooner.

quiz.prompt: We ___ (need) have reserved a table; the restaurant was almost empty.
quiz.why: “Needn't have + V3” = an unnecessary action that still happened; the empty restaurant confirms the booking wasn't required.

quiz.prompt: The delay ___ have been caused by heavy traffic, though we can't be certain.
quiz.why: “May well have + V3” hedges a plausible but uncertain past cause.

quiz.prompt: Current trends suggest that automation ___ (well) replace many routine jobs within a decade.
quiz.why: “May well” hedges a confident-but-cautious prediction, softer than a bare “will”.

quiz.prompt: He ___ have seen the email — he replied to it within a minute.
quiz.why: Must have + V3 expresses a confident positive deduction, supported by the quick reply.

quiz.prompt: The data ___ (seem) to support the hypothesis, although the sample size is small.
quiz.why: “Would seem to” hedges an inference drawn cautiously from limited data.

quiz.prompt: I ___ (should) have double-checked the figures before submitting the report.
quiz.why: “Should have + V3” criticises the speaker's own past inaction.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite using "shouldn't have": "It was a mistake for the team to ignore the warning signs."
answers: The team shouldn't have ignored the warning signs.
The team shouldn't have ignored the warning signs
quiz.why: “Shouldn't have + V3” criticises the team's past action of ignoring the warnings.

title: Participle Clauses
blurb: Reduce relative clauses and combine ideas efficiently with participle clauses.

lesson.body: Participle clauses use -ing, -ed, or a perfect participle instead of a full relative or adverbial clause, producing more concise, sophisticated sentences. They can replace relative clauses or express time, reason, and sequence.

lesson.title: Reduced relative clauses
lesson.body: An active relative clause (who/which + verb) can reduce to a present participle: Passengers who are waiting → Passengers waiting for the delayed train grew frustrated. A passive relative clause reduces to a past participle: The report that was written by the committee → The report written by the committee was published.

lesson.title: Time, reason & result
lesson.body: A participle clause can replace an adverbial clause of time or reason when the subjects match: After she finished the exam, she left → Having finished the exam, she left. Because he did not know the answer, he guessed → Not knowing the answer, he guessed.

lesson.title: Perfect participle for earlier action
lesson.body: The perfect participle (having + V3) shows the participle-clause action happened before the main clause, avoiding ambiguity about sequence: Having submitted the application, she waited anxiously for a reply.

head: Full clause
Participle clause
rows: The man who lives next door is a doctor.
The man living next door is a doctor.
The bridge that was built in 1990 needs repair.
The bridge built in 1990 needs repair.
Because she was unsure, she asked for help.
After he had checked the figures, he signed the report.
Having checked the figures, he signed the report.

items.en: Students studying abroad often adapt quickly to new cultures.
items.note: Reduced active relative clause: “who are studying” → “studying”.

items.en: The data collected during the survey revealed a clear pattern.
items.note: Reduced passive relative clause: “that was collected” → “collected”.

items.en: Realising her mistake, she corrected the report immediately.
items.note: A participle clause of reason replaces “Because she realised…”.

items.en: Having reviewed the evidence, the committee reached its decision.
items.note: The perfect participle shows the review happened before the decision.

items.en: Written in plain language, the guide is accessible to non-specialists.
items.note: A past participle clause fronted for emphasis replaces “Because it is written…”.

lesson.body: Participle clauses let you avoid a string of short clauses joined by “and” or “because”, raising sentence variety for band 8+ writing. Use the perfect participle (having + V3) whenever the order of events matters, to avoid ambiguity.

quiz.prompt: ___ the deadline, the team worked through the weekend.
quiz.why: The active participle “Facing” replaces “Because the team was facing the deadline”.

quiz.prompt: The proposal ___ by the board last week has been sent for review.
quiz.why: A passive relative clause (“that was approved”) reduces to the past participle “approved”.

quiz.prompt: ___ her research, she moved on to writing the conclusion.
quiz.why: The perfect participle “Having completed” shows the research finished before the writing began.

quiz.prompt: Passengers ___ (wait) for the delayed flight filled the departure lounge.
quiz.why: A reduced active relative clause: “who were waiting” → “waiting”.

quiz.prompt: ___ that the results were inconclusive, the researchers repeated the experiment.
quiz.why: The active participle “Realising” replaces “Because they realised…”.

quiz.prompt: The bridge ___ (build) in the nineteenth century still carries heavy traffic today.
quiz.why: A reduced passive relative clause: “that was built” → “built”.

quiz.prompt: ___ enough evidence, the police were unable to make an arrest.
quiz.why: The negative perfect participle places “not” before “having”: “Not having enough evidence…”.

quiz.prompt: ___ (write) in clear, accessible language, the report reached a wide audience.
quiz.why: A fronted past participle clause of reason replaces “Because it was written…”.

quiz.prompt: ___ (finish) the interviews, the panel began deliberating immediately.
quiz.why: The perfect participle shows the interviews were completed before the deliberation began.

quiz.prompt: Combine into one sentence using a participle clause: "She had reviewed all the applications. Then she shortlisted five candidates."
answers: Having reviewed all the applications, she shortlisted five candidates.
Having reviewed all the applications, she shortlisted five candidates
quiz.why: “Having reviewed + V3” shows the review was completed before the shortlisting.

title: Nominalisation
blurb: Convert verbs and adjectives into nouns for a more formal, academic register.

lesson.body: Nominalisation turns a verb or adjective into an abstract noun (increase, decrease, implementation, availability), letting a whole clause become a compact noun phrase. It is one of the clearest markers of formal academic writing, common in Task 1 reports and Task 2 essays.

lesson.title: Verb → noun
lesson.body: Many verbs have a related noun formed with suffixes such as -tion, -sion, -ment, -ance, -al: reduce → reduction, implement → implementation, arrive → arrival, perform → performance. The verb clause Prices increased sharply becomes the noun phrase a sharp increase in prices.

lesson.title: Adjective → noun
lesson.body: Adjectives nominalise with suffixes such as -ity, -ness, -cy: available → availability, efficient → efficiency, accurate → accuracy. The data is not accurate becomes the inaccuracy of the data.

lesson.title: Building formal sentences with nominalisation
lesson.body: Nominalised nouns often become the subject of a formal sentence, paired with a general verb such as lead to, result in, contribute to: The introduction of new regulations led to a decline in emissions. This packs two ideas into one clause instead of two.

head: Verb/adjective
Nominalised form
Example
rows: the implementation of the new policy

items.en: The government's implementation of the new tax policy caused public debate.
items.note: “Implement” → “implementation” becomes the subject noun phrase.

items.en: A steady increase in life expectancy has changed retirement planning.
items.note: “Increase” as a noun avoids the clause “life expectancy increased”.

items.en: The availability of clean water varies significantly between regions.
items.note: “Available” → “availability” as an abstract noun subject.

items.en: The reduction in manufacturing jobs contributed to regional unemployment.
items.note: “Reduce” → “reduction” packages the cause into a noun phrase.

items.en: Improved efficiency in production methods lowered overall costs.
items.note: “Efficient” → “efficiency” as the subject of the sentence.

lesson.body: For band 7+ writing, overusing nominalisation can sound dense and impersonal — balance nominalised sentences with clearer verb-based ones so the essay remains readable, especially in the conclusion where clarity matters most.

quiz.prompt: The government's ___ of the new curriculum faced criticism from teachers.
quiz.why: “Implement” nominalises to “implementation”, the noun form used as the subject.

quiz.prompt: The ___ of affordable housing remains a major issue in the city.
quiz.why: “Available” nominalises to “availability”, the abstract noun needed as the subject.

quiz.prompt: A sharp ___ in oil prices affected transport costs nationwide.
quiz.why: “Increase” functions as a noun here, following the adjective “sharp”.

quiz.prompt: The ___ (reduce) in carbon emissions was smaller than scientists had hoped.
quiz.why: “Reduce” nominalises to “reduction”, the noun form used as the subject.

quiz.prompt: Improvements in production ___ helped the company cut costs significantly.
quiz.why: “Efficient” nominalises to “efficiency”, the noun needed after “production”.

quiz.prompt: The company's ___ (arrive) in the Asian market boosted its annual revenue.
quiz.why: “Arrive” nominalises to “arrival”, the noun form used as the subject.

quiz.prompt: The ___ of the data was questioned after several errors were found.
quiz.why: “Accurate” nominalises to the uncountable noun “accuracy”.

quiz.prompt: The committee's ___ (decide) to postpone the vote surprised many members.
quiz.why: “Decide” nominalises to “decision”, the noun form used as the subject.

quiz.prompt: ___ (perform) in the exam depends heavily on effective time management.
quiz.why: “Perform” nominalises to “performance”, the noun form used as the subject.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite using a nominalised form of "increase": "Prices increased sharply after the storm."
answers: There was a sharp increase in prices after the storm.
There was a sharp increase in prices after the storm
A sharp increase in prices followed the storm.
A sharp increase in prices followed the storm
quiz.why: “Increase” as a noun packages the verb clause into a noun phrase, typical of formal academic style.

title: Hedging & Academic Tone
blurb: Soften claims and avoid overgeneralisation with hedging verbs, adverbs and quantifiers.

lesson.body: Hedging softens a claim so it sounds cautious and evidence-based rather than absolute — essential for academic register, where over-confident generalisations (all, always, definitely) weaken an argument's credibility.

lesson.title: Hedging verbs
lesson.body: Verbs such as suggest, indicate, appear, seem, tend to present a claim as likely rather than certain: The data suggests a correlation, not necessarily causation. Younger respondents tend to favour online shopping.

lesson.title: Hedging adverbs & quantifiers
lesson.body: Adverbs like arguably, largely, broadly, potentially and quantifiers like a number of, many, to some extent avoid overgeneralisation: The policy is arguably the most effective option. To some extent, both factors contributed.

lesson.title: Avoiding absolutes
lesson.body: Replace absolute words (all, every, always, never, prove) with cautious alternatives (most, many, often, rarely, suggest) unless the claim is genuinely universal: Most studies indicate… rather than All studies prove…, which a single counter-example could disprove.

head: Overstated (risky)
Hedged (academic)
rows: Many students appear to prefer online learning.
This proves the theory is correct.
This suggests the theory may be correct.
The policy will definitely reduce poverty.
The policy could potentially reduce poverty.

items.en: The survey results suggest a shift in consumer preferences.
items.note: “Suggest” hedges the claim rather than stating it as fact.

items.en: Remote work appears to increase productivity for some employees.
items.note: “Appears to” and “for some” avoid overgeneralisation.

items.en: The findings are arguably the most significant of the decade.
items.note: “Arguably” signals a defensible opinion, not an absolute fact.

items.en: A number of factors may have contributed to the decline.
items.note: “A number of” and “may have” hedge both quantity and certainty.

items.en: This trend is likely, to some extent, to continue in the coming years.
items.note: “To some extent” limits the scope of the claim.

lesson.body: For band 8+ Writing Task 2, hedge your thesis and key claims but not your structure signposts — reserve confident language (clearly, this essay will argue) for organisation, and hedges (arguably, tends to) for the substance of your claims.

quiz.prompt: The results ___ a link between diet and concentration, though further research is needed.
quiz.why: “Suggest” hedges the claim as likely rather than certain.

quiz.prompt: ___ speaking, urban residents report higher stress levels than rural residents.
quiz.why: “Broadly speaking” hedges a generalisation without claiming it applies to every case.

quiz.prompt: Which sentence is best hedged for an academic essay?
options: Many commuters express dissatisfaction with public transport.
quiz.why: “Many” and “express dissatisfaction” avoid the absolute, unsupportable claim made by “all/every/always”.

quiz.prompt: The data ___ (indicate) a modest improvement in reading scores.
quiz.why: “Indicate(s)” hedges the claim as likely rather than certain; “data” is treated as plural in formal style, though “indicates” is common too.

quiz.prompt: Younger employees ___ to prefer flexible working arrangements these days.
quiz.why: “Tend to” hedges a generalisation; the plural subject “employees” takes “tend”.

quiz.prompt: ___ (large), the policy has been well received, though some critics disagree.
quiz.why: “Largely” hedges the claim, acknowledging it is not universally true.

quiz.prompt: The proposal is ___ (argue) the most cost-effective solution available.
quiz.why: “Arguably” signals a defensible opinion rather than an absolute fact.

quiz.prompt: To some ___, both economic and cultural factors explain the trend.
quiz.why: “To some extent” is a fixed hedging phrase limiting the scope of a claim.

quiz.prompt: The report notes that the reform ___ (potential) reduces inequality rather than eliminates it.
quiz.why: “Potentially” hedges the claim, avoiding the overstated verb “eliminates”.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite to hedge this overstated claim: "This proves that social media causes anxiety in teenagers."
answers: This suggests that social media may cause anxiety in teenagers.
This suggests that social media may cause anxiety in teenagers
This suggests that social media could cause anxiety in teenagers.
This suggests that social media could cause anxiety in teenagers
quiz.why: “Suggests” and “may/could” replace the absolute claim “proves” with a cautious, evidence-based one.

title: Mixed Conditionals
blurb: Combine different time frames in one conditional sentence for nuanced cause-and-effect.

lesson.body: A mixed conditional combines an if-clause and a main clause from different time frames, linking a past cause to a present result, or a present/general truth to a past result — beyond the standard first-third conditional pairings.

lesson.title: Past cause → present result
lesson.body: If + past perfect, would + base form links an unreal past action to a present consequence: If she had studied medicine, she would be a doctor now. The past action didn't happen, and its present effect is also unreal.

lesson.title: Present/general cause → past result
lesson.body: If + past simple, would have + past participle links an ongoing present truth (usually about character or state) to an unreal past result: If he weren't so stubborn, he wouldn't have argued with the referee. The present state is real and ongoing; the past result is unreal.

lesson.title: Choosing the right mix
lesson.body: Ask which clause is unreal now and which was unreal then. A present state (if I were more organised) needs would have + V3 for the past result; a past action (if I had left earlier) needs would + base for the present result.

head: If-clause (time)
Main clause (time)
Example
rows: If they had missed the flight, they would be stuck at the airport now.
If I weren't so busy, I would have joined you yesterday.

items.en: If she had accepted the job offer, she would be living in Singapore now.
items.note: Past cause (unreal) → present result (unreal).

items.en: If he weren't afraid of flying, he would have visited his family last year.
items.note: Present ongoing state → unreal past result.

items.en: If the bridge hadn't been built, the two towns would still be isolated.
items.note: Past cause → present result, with a passive if-clause.

items.en: If I spoke French fluently, I would have applied for that job in Paris.
items.note: Present state (I don't speak French) → unreal past result.

items.en: If the company had invested in training, staff turnover would be much lower today.
items.note: Past cause → ongoing present result.

lesson.body: Mixed conditionals let you show precisely how a past decision still affects the present, or how a stable trait shaped a specific past event — a nuance basic conditionals cannot express, and one examiners notice in band 8+ responses.

quiz.prompt: If she ___ harder at school, she would be at university now.
quiz.why: Past cause (unreal past action) takes “if + past perfect”: “had studied”.

quiz.prompt: If he weren't so shy, he ___ to more social events last year.
quiz.why: A present ongoing state (“weren't so shy”) links to an unreal past result: “would have gone”.

quiz.prompt: If the team ___ better in the first half, they would be leading the tournament now.
quiz.why: Past cause (unreal past action) takes “if + past perfect”: “had played”.

quiz.prompt: If I ___ (be) more careful with money, I wouldn't have gone into debt last year.
quiz.why: A present ongoing trait (carelessness with money) links to an unreal past result; “were” is the traditional form, “was” a common informal alternative.

quiz.prompt: If they hadn't sold the house, they ___ so much space now.
quiz.why: Past cause (unreal past action) links to a present result: “would have” + noun.

quiz.prompt: If the government ___ (invest) more in public transport a decade ago, congestion wouldn't be such a problem today.
quiz.why: Past cause (unreal past action) takes “if + past perfect”: “had invested”, linked to a present result.

quiz.prompt: If we hadn't missed the earlier train, we ___ stuck at the station now.
quiz.why: Past cause (unreal past action) links to an unreal present result: “wouldn't be”.

quiz.prompt: If he ___ (not / lose) his passport, he would be on holiday in Spain right now.
quiz.why: Past cause (unreal past action) takes “if + past perfect”: “hadn't lost”, linked to a present result.

quiz.prompt: If I ___ (know) how to swim, I would have joined the rescue team yesterday.
quiz.why: A present ongoing state (not knowing how to swim) links to an unreal past result: “would have joined”.

quiz.prompt: Combine into a mixed conditional (past cause, present result): "She didn't take the internship. She isn't working in finance now."
answers: If she had taken the internship, she would be working in finance now.
If she had taken the internship, she would be working in finance now
quiz.why: A past unreal cause (didn't take the internship) plus a present unreal result (isn't working in finance) needs “if + past perfect, would + base”.

title: Ellipsis & Substitution
blurb: Avoid repetition with ellipsis and substitution words like one, do so, and so/not.

lesson.body: Ellipsis omits words that are recoverable from context; substitution replaces them with a short word such as one, do so, so, not. Both make writing and speech more economical and cohesive, avoiding clumsy repetition.

lesson.title: Ellipsis after auxiliaries
lesson.body: Repeated verb phrases after auxiliaries can be omitted: She can speak French, and he can [speak French] too. → She can speak French, and he can too. This is common after and, but, if, though.

lesson.title: Substitution with one/ones and do so
lesson.body: One/ones replaces a countable noun already mentioned: I need a pen — do you have one? Do so/did so substitutes for a whole action already named, avoiding repetition of the main verb: She submitted her essay before the deadline; he did so as well.

lesson.title: So/not after belief verbs
lesson.body: After verbs like think, hope, believe, suppose, expect, so substitutes for a whole positive clause and not (or don't think so) for a negative one: Will the plan succeed? I hope so. / I hope not.

head: Device
Replaces
Example
rows: She has finished, and I have too.
This report is better than the last one.
He revised thoroughly; she did so too.
Is the meeting cancelled? I believe so.

items.en: He hasn't submitted his assignment, but she has.
items.note: Ellipsis omits the repeated verb phrase “submitted her assignment”.

items.en: The new proposal is more detailed than the previous one.
items.note: “One” substitutes for “proposal”, avoiding repetition.

items.en: The manager approved the budget, and the board did so unanimously.
items.note: “Did so” substitutes for the whole action “approved the budget”.

items.en: Will costs keep rising? Economists suspect so.
items.note: “So” replaces the whole clause “that costs will keep rising”.

items.en: Is the project on schedule? I'm afraid not.
items.note: “Not” substitutes for the negative clause after a belief verb.

lesson.body: In Speaking Part 3, short substitutions like I think so, I hope not sound natural and fluent; in Writing, ellipsis after auxiliaries (…, and so did the profits) avoids clunky repetition without sacrificing precision.

quiz.prompt: She hasn't finished the report, but he ___.
quiz.why: Ellipsis omits the repeated verb phrase “finished the report” after the auxiliary “has”.

quiz.prompt: Do you need a laptop, or do you already have ___?
quiz.why: “One” substitutes for the countable noun “a laptop” already mentioned.

quiz.prompt: Will the negotiations succeed? I really hope ___.
quiz.why: “So” substitutes for the whole positive clause after the belief verb “hope”.

quiz.prompt: He revised his essay carefully, and his classmates ___ (do) too.
quiz.why: Ellipsis with “did too” substitutes for the repeated action “revised their essays carefully”.

quiz.prompt: Is the flight delayed? I'm afraid ___.
quiz.why: “Not” substitutes for the negative clause after the belief verb “afraid”.

quiz.prompt: The results were disappointing, and so ___ the team's morale.
quiz.why: “So” + auxiliary + subject shows a shared quality; “morale” is singular, so “was”.

quiz.prompt: I don't think the plan will work, but my colleague thinks ___.
quiz.why: “So” substitutes for the whole positive clause after the belief verb “thinks”.

quiz.prompt: The first draft was too long; the revised ___ is much clearer.
quiz.why: “One” substitutes for the countable noun “draft” already mentioned.

quiz.prompt: She apologised for the delay, and so ___ her manager.
quiz.why: “So” + auxiliary + subject shows a shared action; the past tense auxiliary “did” substitutes for “apologised”.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite using ellipsis to avoid repeating the verb phrase: "My sister can drive a car, and my brother can drive a car too."
answers: My sister can drive a car, and my brother can too.
My sister can drive a car, and my brother can too
quiz.why: Ellipsis omits the repeated verb phrase “drive a car” after the auxiliary “can”.

title: Dummy Subjects (it/there)
blurb: Use 'it' and 'there' as dummy subjects, and shift long subject clauses to the end with extraposition.

lesson.body: English disfavours long, heavy subjects. It and there can act as empty “dummy” subjects, allowing the real information to move later in the sentence — a technique called extraposition, common in formal writing.

lesson.title: Dummy it
lesson.body: It stands in for time, weather, distance, or a whole clause: It is important that funding be increased. It takes years to master a language. It is worth noting that the sample size was small.

lesson.title: Dummy there
lesson.body: There introduces the existence of something, with the real subject after be: There is likely to be a shortage of housing. There tends to be less demand in winter. The verb agrees with the noun after be, not with there.

lesson.title: Extraposition
lesson.body: A long clausal subject usually moves to the end, replaced by it: That she will succeed is unlikely (heavy, unusual) → It is unlikely that she will succeed (natural). This keeps the sentence opening light and easy to process.

head: Pattern
Example
rows: It is essential that the data be verified.
It took the team three months to finish.
There is likely to be a delay.
It is surprising how quickly attitudes changed.

items.en: It is unlikely that the policy will be reversed before the election.
items.note: Extraposition: the heavy that-clause moves after “it is unlikely”.

items.en: It took researchers five years to develop the vaccine.
items.note: The “it + take + time + to-infinitive” pattern.

items.en: There is likely to be a significant rise in demand next quarter.
items.note: “There” introduces existence; “be” agrees with “a significant rise”.

items.en: There tend to be more applicants in the final year of the course.
items.note: “There tend to be” agrees with the plural “applicants”.

items.en: It is worth remembering that correlation does not imply causation.
items.note: “It is worth + -ing” introduces a cautionary comment.

lesson.body: Extraposition (It is clear that…, It is worth noting that…) is one of the simplest, most reliable ways to open a formal sentence in Task 2 without an awkward, over-long subject clause.

quiz.prompt: ___ is essential that the report be submitted by Friday.
quiz.why: Dummy “it” introduces the extraposed that-clause after “is essential”.

quiz.prompt: ___ took the committee almost a year to reach a decision.
quiz.why: Dummy “it” is used with “take + time + to-infinitive”.

quiz.prompt: ___ is likely to be a shortage of qualified teachers in rural areas.
quiz.why: Dummy “there” introduces the existence of a shortage; the real subject follows “be”.

quiz.prompt: ___ (there) tend to be fewer applicants during the summer months.
quiz.why: Dummy “there” introduces existence, agreeing with the plural noun “applicants” after “be”.

quiz.prompt: Which sentence uses extraposition correctly?
options: That she left early was surprising.
It was surprising that she left early.
Surprising was that she left early.
quiz.why: Extraposition moves the heavy clausal subject after “it was surprising”, the natural formal pattern.

quiz.prompt: It is worth ___ (note) that the two studies used different methodologies.
quiz.why: “It is worth + -ing” introduces a cautionary or notable comment.

quiz.prompt: ___ is no doubt that renewable energy will play a larger role in future policy.
quiz.why: “There is no doubt that…” is a fixed dummy-there pattern introducing a clause.

quiz.prompt: It ___ (take) the researchers over a decade to complete the longitudinal study.
quiz.why: “It + take + time + to-infinitive” describes how long an action required.

quiz.prompt: ___ (it) is surprising how quickly public opinion shifted on this issue.
quiz.why: Dummy “it” introduces the extraposed clause “how quickly public opinion shifted”.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite using extraposition with 'it': "That the experiment failed twice was disappointing."
answers: It was disappointing that the experiment failed twice.
It was disappointing that the experiment failed twice
quiz.why: Extraposition moves the heavy clausal subject after “it was disappointing”.

title: Fronting & -ever Clauses
blurb: Front elements for emphasis and use -ever clauses to express 'no matter' concessions.

lesson.body: Fronting moves an element (usually an adverbial, adjective, or object) to the start of a sentence for emphasis or to link back to previous information. -ever clauses (whatever, whoever, however, wherever, whenever) express a “no matter” concession, a common academic device for anticipating exceptions.

lesson.title: Fronting adverbials & complements
lesson.body: An adverbial or adjective complement can open the sentence for emphasis, often followed by inversion if the subject is a full noun phrase: Equally important is the need for clear regulation. Especially striking was the rate of change.

lesson.title: Fronting objects
lesson.body: An object can be fronted to link to something already mentioned or to contrast it: This argument, however, few economists accept. Such claims, critics argue, lack sufficient evidence.

lesson.title: -ever concessive clauses
lesson.body: Whatever/whoever/however/wherever/whenever + clause means “it does not matter which/who/how…”: Whatever the outcome, the team has worked hard. However difficult the exam is, most candidates pass eventually. However + adjective/adverb + subject + verb is a fixed word order — not “however the exam is difficult”.

head: Device
Example
rows: Equally significant is the drop in exports.
Whatever the reason, the deadline stands.
However hard she tried, the result was the same.

items.en: Especially concerning is the decline in youth employment.
items.note: A fronted adjective complement, followed by inversion (“is” + subject).

items.en: This conclusion, however, not everyone accepts.
items.note: A fronted object “this conclusion” links back to the previous point.

items.en: Whatever the cause, the effect on local wildlife has been severe.
items.note: “Whatever” concedes the cause is unknown or irrelevant to the point.

items.en: However carefully the survey was designed, some bias was unavoidable.
items.note: “However” + adverb + subject + verb; note the fixed word order.

items.en: Wherever the policy has been trialled, results have been mixed.
items.note: “Wherever” concedes location does not change the outcome.

lesson.body: Fronting and -ever clauses both signal sophistication, but use them sparingly — one per paragraph is usually enough. “However” as a concessive conjunction (however hard…) must not be confused with “however” as a contrast adverb (However, the results were mixed.).

quiz.prompt: Especially ___ is the sharp rise in youth unemployment.
quiz.why: The fronted adjective complement “concerning” is followed by inversion (“is” + subject).

quiz.prompt: ___ the outcome of the vote, the policy debate will continue.
quiz.why: “Whatever” + noun concedes that the outcome does not change the following statement.

quiz.prompt: ___ difficult the exam was, nearly all candidates passed.
quiz.why: “However” + adjective + subject + verb is the fixed concessive pattern for degree.

quiz.prompt: ___ (who) wins the election, the new policy will take effect in January.
quiz.why: “Whoever” concedes that the identity of the winner does not change the outcome.

quiz.prompt: This theory, ___, few scientists now support.
quiz.why: The fronted object “this theory” is followed by the contrast adverb “however” before the subject “few scientists”.

quiz.prompt: ___ (where) the factory is built, local residents will be affected by the noise.
quiz.why: “Wherever” concedes that the location does not change the outcome.

quiz.prompt: Equally ___ is the need for stronger enforcement of the regulations.
quiz.why: The fronted adjective complement “important” is followed by inversion (“is” + subject).

quiz.prompt: ___ (when) the meeting is rescheduled, at least three members will be unavailable.
quiz.why: “Whenever” concedes that the timing does not change the outcome.

quiz.prompt: This claim, ___ (few) would seriously question, has shaped policy for a decade.
quiz.why: The fronted object “this claim” is followed by the parenthetical subject “few” and verb “would seriously question”.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite fronting the object for emphasis: "Few researchers would dispute this finding."
answers: This finding, few researchers would dispute.
This finding few researchers would dispute.
quiz.why: Fronting the object “this finding” places emphasis on it and links back to earlier discussion.

title: Gradable Adjectives & Intensifiers
blurb: Distinguish gradable from extreme adjectives, choose the right intensifier, and use -ed/-ing adjectives correctly.

lesson.body: Gradable adjectives (big, good, interesting) describe qualities that exist in degrees and combine with intensifiers like very. Extreme/non-gradable adjectives (huge, excellent, fascinating) already express the top of a scale and take different intensifiers. -ed/-ing adjectives (bored/boring) describe feeling versus cause.

lesson.title: Gradable vs extreme adjectives
lesson.body: Gradable adjectives take very, quite, fairly, extremely, rather: very interesting, fairly difficult. Extreme adjectives (huge, superb, exhausted, freezing, terrified) already mean “very + gradable” and instead take absolutely, completely, utterly, really: absolutely exhausted, not very exhausted.

lesson.title: Choosing the right intensifier
lesson.body: Very does not naturally collocate with extreme adjectives (very huge sounds odd); absolutely does not naturally collocate with plain gradable adjectives (absolutely big sounds odd). Really works with both types.

lesson.title: -ed vs -ing adjectives
lesson.body: -ed adjectives describe how someone feels: The students were bored. -ing adjectives describe the thing or situation causing that feeling: The lecture was boring. Using the wrong form (I am boring instead of I am bored) reverses the meaning.

items.en: The exam was extremely difficult, but not impossibly so.
items.note: “Extremely” intensifies the gradable adjective “difficult”.

items.en: After the twelve-hour flight, we were absolutely exhausted.
items.note: “Absolutely” collocates with the extreme adjective “exhausted”, not “very”.

items.en: It was freezing outside, so we stayed indoors all afternoon.
items.note: “Freezing” is already an extreme adjective meaning “very cold”.

items.en: The lecture was so boring that several students fell asleep.
items.note: The “-ing” adjective describes the cause of the feeling.

items.en: I felt genuinely fascinated by the museum's new exhibition.
items.note: The “-ed” adjective describes the visitor's feeling: “fascinated”, not “fascinating”.

lesson.body: Mixing up very with extreme adjectives (very huge) or -ed/-ing adjectives (I'm boring) is a very common error that stands out even in otherwise fluent band 7 responses — precise intensifier and -ed/-ing choices are an easy way to signal band 8+ control.

quiz.prompt: After running the marathon, she was ___ exhausted.
quiz.why: “Exhausted” is an extreme adjective and collocates with “absolutely”, not the gradable intensifier “very”.

quiz.prompt: The lake was ___ freezing; we could see ice forming at the edges.
quiz.why: “Freezing” is an extreme adjective and collocates with “absolutely”, not gradable intensifiers like “very”.

quiz.prompt: The presentation was very ___, though not the worst I've seen.
quiz.why: “Rather” pairs naturally with the gradable adjective “dull”; extreme adjectives like “terrible” or “appalling” already denote the top of the scale.

quiz.prompt: I am not ___ (bore) by the topic; I actually find it quite interesting.
quiz.why: The “-ed” adjective “bored” describes how the speaker feels.

quiz.prompt: Which sentence uses the intensifier correctly?
quiz.why: “Fascinating” is an extreme adjective and collocates with “absolutely”, not moderating intensifiers like “very”, “fairly”, or “pretty”.

quiz.prompt: The children found the puppet show extremely ___ (amuse).
quiz.why: The “-ing” adjective “amusing” describes the cause of the children's amusement.

quiz.prompt: We were ___ terrified during the turbulence on the flight.
quiz.why: “Terrified” is an extreme adjective and collocates with “utterly”, not gradable intensifiers like “very”.

quiz.prompt: She looked ___ (fascinate) by the ancient manuscripts on display.
quiz.why: The “-ed” adjective “fascinated” describes how she felt.

quiz.prompt: The marathon route was ___ (exhaust) even for experienced runners.
quiz.why: The “-ing” adjective “exhausting” describes the effect the route had on the runners.

quiz.prompt: Rewrite replacing 'very' with a more natural intensifier for this extreme adjective: "The results were very astonishing."
quiz.why: “Astonishing” is an extreme adjective; it collocates with “absolutely/utterly/completely”, not the gradable intensifier “very”.
